does: This paper introduces OdysSim, a systematic framework for training foundation models specifically designed to simulate human behavior rather than serve as helpful assistants. The authors develop SOUL, a taxonomy organizing behavioral simulation into five capability axes—conversation, social sensitivity, cognition, role-playing, and evaluation—that unifies 62 datasets and 23 benchmark tasks. They create a 21.4M interaction corpus with back-generated social contexts totaling 10B tokens, and propose an end-to-end training recipe combining midtraining on behavioral data, task-specific reinforcement learning, and expert distillation. The resulting 8B parameter OSim model achieves first or tied-first performance on 8 of 23 benchmarks, outperforming individual frontier models while producing outputs with more realistic length, formatting, and word distributions that better capture authentic human communication patterns.
connects: This work addresses a known limitation of post-trained large language models: their tendency toward homogeneous, overly agreeable outputs that diverge from authentic human behavior. It builds on prior research using LLMs as human simulators for interactive evaluation and social simulation, extending this line by systematically characterizing what behavioral simulation requires. The paper connects to work on behavioral modeling, multi-task learning frameworks, and reinforcement learning from human feedback, while responding to the Sim2Real gap problem—where simulation outputs diverge from real human behavior. The taxonomy-driven approach follows emerging patterns in foundation model evaluation frameworks that organize capabilities across multiple dimensions rather than optimizing for narrow metrics.
enables: This framework enables more realistic human behavior simulation for interactive evaluation, social science research, and applications requiring authentic behavioral diversity. The open OSim model and SOUL taxonomy provide tools for future researchers to systematically develop and benchmark behavioral simulators beyond the helpful assistant paradigm. The modular training recipe combining midtraining, RL, and distillation can be adapted to other behavioral domains or extended to larger model scales. The work opens questions about scaling behavioral foundation models, incorporating demographic and cultural diversity in simulations, and validating simulation fidelity against real-world data. It also points toward applications in interactive testing of social systems, policy simulation, and human-AI interaction design where behavioral authenticity rather than helpfulness is paramount.
